---
title: "Florida Lupine (Lupinus floridanus)"
slug: "lupinus-floridanus"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622262712/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Florida Lupine"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fabales"
tax_family: "Fabaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fabales", "Fabaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622262712/medium.jpg" alt="Florida Lupine" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/dsandii">
dsandii</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341778531">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fabales
-   **Family:** Fabaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=1525187)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A showy wildflower characterized by its palmately compound leaves and spikes of blue to purple pea-like flowers. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, sandhills, and open pine flatwoods, particularly in central regions. It typically blooms in spring and, as a legume, helps enrich the soil through nitrogen fixation while also attracting pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1525187-Lupinus-floridanus)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/genus/728)
